figures.caption: Table 1: GPQA Diamond results grouped by prover. HC-Cov: fraction of questions flagged highconfidence. HC-Prec: accuracy on that subset. Gap: HC-Prec minus accuracy on the complement. Calls: mean LLM calls per question. ∗: SC with extended thinking (Epoch AI benchmark); not directly comparable to PVD runs (standard API). †: challenge-first verifier prompt. ‡: HC coverage >90%, leaving n<20 in the complement; gap estimate unreliable.

Figure 1: Cost–precision tradeoff on GPQA Diamond for the clean RTTR runs. Each point is a method; the y-axis shows HC-Prec (accuracy on the reported high-confidence subset), the x-axis shows mean cents per question on a log scale, and bubble area encodes HC-Cov (fraction of questions reported). PVD variants occupy the high-precision region while exposing a controllable cost–coverage tradeoff: stricter or retry-based protocols spend more compute to report on more questions, while lower-cost variants abstain more aggressively.
Table 4: Effect of verifier choice on ANC calibration, holding prover fixed. Avg R: mean challenge rounds. †: challenge-first prompt. Self : same model as prover (single-call ablation).

Table 3: Humanity’s Last Exam results (GPT-5.5 prover, Gemini 3.1 Pro verifier, n=513, T=12, K=1). Rows sorted by n. HC-Cov = Pr[ANC]; HC-Prec = Pr[correct | ANC]; Gap: HC-Prec minus non-ANC accuracy.

Table 7: Clean RTTR runs on GPQA Diamond (n=198) with 95% confidence intervals and a gap significance test. Acc, HC-Cov, and HC-Prec use Wilson score intervals; Gap (HC-Prec minus non-HC accuracy) uses a percentile bootstrap (10,000 resamples). Gap p is a two-sided Fisher’s exact test of H0: HC and non-HC subsets are equally accurate. A Gap CI excluding 0 (equivalently p < 0.05) indicates the high-confidence subset is significantly more accurate than its complement. ∗: SC with extended thinking (Epoch AI benchmark).
